title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a local museum. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Nina, The Exhibition Guide
Nina joined the museum as a weekend guide after moving to the town and wanting a reason to meet people. She expected to repeat facts from a card, but the work demanded much more attention to visitors’ reactions, mood and pace. [47]After several guests walked past the same display without understanding where to go, she helped redesign the small direction labels between the rooms.[/47] [48]Her greatest early worry was speaking without a full script, especially when someone asked a question she had not prepared for.[/48] She soon learned to admit uncertainty and check details later, which visitors seemed to appreciate. The most satisfying days were when teenagers stayed longer than their teachers had planned because an object connected with music or sport caught their imagination. Nina now thinks a guide’s real task is not to deliver a lecture, but to notice when curiosity is beginning and keep it alive.
texts.B:
### Paul, The Archive Assistant
Paul spent Tuesday evenings in the museum archive, sorting boxes that had been donated by local families and small businesses. He enjoyed the silence, although the dust made him sneeze for the first month of winter. [49]He was shocked to find that damp cardboard had damaged letters which should have been stored flat in acid-free folders.[/49] That discovery made him push for a small grant to buy proper storage materials. [50]His former job in a public library helped him recognise old cataloguing marks and estimate when several undated photographs had been printed.[/50] He also became unexpectedly good at reading handwriting that seemed impossible at first glance. Paul says the slow work suited him because every envelope might contain a forgotten clue about the town and its families. His favourite find was a programme from a cinema that closed before he was born, with a child’s ticket still tucked inside.
texts.C:
### Greta, The School Workshop Leader
Greta began helping with school workshops after the education curator heard her telling stories at a local book festival. [51]The curator invited her to create sessions that would make the museum’s oldest objects less intimidating for younger children.[/51] Greta decided to let pupils handle safe replicas before seeing the originals behind glass, so their first response was curiosity rather than fear. The idea worked, but the sessions were rarely calm during school mornings. [52]She found the hardest part was keeping excited groups from shouting over one another while still allowing them to explore freely.[/52] She experimented patiently with bells, picture cards and short challenges until the rhythm finally felt manageable. Greta was surprised by how quickly children invented uses for objects whose real purpose they had not yet discovered. She now believes workshops should leave space for wrong guesses, because those guesses often lead to the most memorable explanations.
texts.D:
### Malik, The Oral History Recorder
Malik volunteered on the oral history project, recording memories from residents who remembered the museum building when it was still a factory. [53]He became involved because his grandfather had worked there as a night watchman and rarely spoke about that period at home.[/53] At first Malik filled every interview with too many questions, afraid that silence meant failure. [54]He gradually learned that short pauses often encouraged people to continue with richer details than any prompt could produce in a hurry.[/54] Some recordings were unexpectedly emotional, especially when interviewees described strikes, weddings and wartime shifts in the same room. Malik valued the technical training he received, but he cared more about protecting each speaker’s voice and permission choices. [55]He is now writing a simple handover guide so future volunteers can continue the recordings consistently.[/55] [56]The best reward was receiving messages from families who recognised relatives in clips posted by the museum.[/56]

questions.question: mentions changing signs because visitors were missing the intended route?
questions.explanation: Nina helped redesign direction labels after guests walked past a display without understanding where to go.

questions.question: admits being nervous when they had to speak without prepared wording?
questions.explanation: Nina worried about speaking without a full script when visitors asked unprepared questions.

questions.question: discovered that poor storage conditions had harmed historical material?
questions.explanation: Paul found damp cardboard had damaged letters that needed better storage.

questions.question: says a previous library role helped them interpret archive material?
questions.explanation: Paul used his library experience to recognise cataloguing marks and date photographs.

questions.question: was invited by a museum employee to make old objects easier for children to approach?
questions.explanation: Greta was invited by the education curator to create sessions for younger children.

questions.question: found it difficult to manage enthusiastic children without stopping their exploration?
questions.explanation: Greta struggled to keep excited groups from shouting while still letting them explore.

questions.question: became involved because of a family connection with the building’s past?
questions.explanation: Malik joined because his grandfather had worked in the building when it was a factory.

questions.question: learned that leaving brief silences could improve people’s answers?
questions.explanation: Malik found that short pauses encouraged interviewees to continue with richer details.

questions.question: is preparing guidance so later volunteers can follow the same method?
questions.explanation: Malik is writing a handover guide for future volunteers.

questions.question: valued hearing from families who recognised people in published recordings?
questions.explanation: Malik valued messages from families who recognised relatives in clips posted by the museum.
